nodes.label: Bus factor of one
nodes.description: The project depends entirely on a single maintainer with no co-maintainer of equal authority.

nodes.label: Maintainer burnout
nodes.description: Chronic exhaustion of the maintainer leading to disengagement.

nodes.label: Missing onboarding doc
nodes.description: No CONTRIBUTING file, no setup guide, no architecture overview for newcomers.

nodes.label: Hard-to-build codebase
nodes.description: First-time contributors cannot get the project running locally in under an hour.

nodes.label: Toxic review culture
nodes.description: Pull requests receive harsh or dismissive feedback that discourages future contributions.

nodes.label: Slow PR reviews
nodes.description: Median time from PR open to first review exceeds two weeks.

nodes.label: Few active contributors
nodes.description: Fewer than three contributors submitting changes over a 90-day window.

nodes.label: Discouraged contributors
nodes.description: Contributors stop opening new pull requests after negative or absent interactions.

nodes.label: Stale issue tracker
nodes.description: Issues remain open for months without triage or response.

nodes.label: Users lose trust
nodes.description: Downstream users perceive the project as unreliable and search for alternatives.

nodes.label: Fork competes
nodes.description: A community fork attracts the active contributor pool away from the original repo.

nodes.label: No release in year
nodes.description: The project has not shipped a new version for more than twelve months.

nodes.label: Security patches lag
nodes.description: Known vulnerabilities are not patched for weeks or months.

nodes.label: Project death
nodes.description: The project is abandoned: no commits, no responses, no users.

nodes.label: CI pipeline
nodes.description: Automated test and build pipeline triggered on each pull request.

nodes.label: Broken CI
nodes.description: CI is red on main branch for an extended period.

nodes.label: Governance model
nodes.description: Documented decision-making process and roles for maintainers and contributors.

nodes.label: Succession plan
nodes.description: Process for transferring maintainership when the current lead steps away.

rels.evidence: When only one person carries the load, fatigue accumulates without relief.
Without setup guides, newcomers struggle to reach a working environment.
Contributors give up before submitting their first PR if the bar to entry is high.
Harsh feedback drives away contributors directly.
Word spreads and prospective contributors avoid the project.
Long waits make contributors feel their work is unwanted.
With fewer reviewers, queues stretch out.
Discouragement directly translates to attrition.
When the contributor base shrinks, the maintainer absorbs more of the workload.
Contributors lose confidence in their changes when the test suite is broken.
A disengaged maintainer reviews less, and slower.
Triage is one of the first activities to drop when burnout sets in.
Unanswered issues read as a project that no longer cares.
Users equate a long release gap with project abandonment.
Unpatched CVEs force downstream users to plan migrations.
Cutting a release requires sustained focus the burnt-out maintainer cannot provide.
Triaging and patching CVEs require energy that burnout depletes first.
Once trust erodes, a fork becomes both attractive and feasible.
A successful fork drains the remaining contributor and user community.
Without the keystone maintainer, the project collapses.
Documented governance is a prerequisite for an orderly handover.
A succession plan allows the project to survive the loss of its original maintainer.
Explicit norms can curb harsh reviewing behavior.
Broken CI presupposes the existence of a CI pipeline to be broken.
Even without build issues, lack of docs reduces the funnel of new contributors.
Concentrating all knowledge in one person makes a single departure terminal.
